summary: The author achieved 5th place by constructing a deliberately diverse ensemble of models spanning retrieval-based classifiers, NLI framing, and large language models. Key technical choices included per-rule specialists, test-time training (TTT) for LLMs and DeBERTa, asymmetric few-shot example selection for a 32B model, and rank-normalized hierarchical blending to stabilize calibration across paradigms. The pipeline emphasized deduplication, de-biasing near-duplicates, and consistent instruction-only prompting with constrained Yes/No decoding.
extensive_summary:
## Overview & Core Strategy
The solution centers on a deliberately diverse ensemble that combines per-rule specialists, inference-only large models, and test-time-trained (TTT) components. Rather than relying on a single architecture, the author blended retrieval-based classifiers, NLI-framed models, and LLMs using per-rule rank-normalized weighting to mitigate calibration drift across different model families.

## Data Preprocessing & Handling
Raw data was cleaned by dropping the `subreddit` field, which was treated as a noisy and unstable signal. Exact duplicates were removed, shrinking the training set to under 10k examples without negatively impacting leaderboard performance. For the embedding pipeline, near-duplicates within each rule were clustered (cosine similarity > 0.95–0.97) and down-weighted to prevent overfitting to repeated patterns.

## Model Architectures & Training Details
The ensemble included several distinct components:
- **LLM TTT:** LLaMA-3.1 8B Instruct and Qwen-3 8B were fine-tuned using LoRA (r=256, alpha=32) with paged_adamw_8bit optimizer and cosine LR scheduling. Training used DDP, fp16, gradient checkpointing, and a 512-sequence length. Inference leveraged vLLM with constrained Yes/No decoding.
- **Embedding Models:** Six families (E5, Qwen3-Embedding, BGE, GTE) were used to train two light heads per rule: Logistic Regression (grid over 31 C values) and distance-weighted KNN (K=10–15). Outputs were blended (0.7 LR + 0.3 KNN) and further ensemble-searched across models via GPU-accelerated simplex optimization.
- **DeBERTa NLI:** A rule-aware zero-shot approach framed the task as NLI (Premise: comment, Hypothesis: violation statement + 8 paraphrases). During TTT, only biases, classifier heads, and LayerNorms were updated on a dynamically balanced rule-specific test set.
- **Qwen-32B Asymmetric Few-Shot:** Contextual examples were selected via cosine similarity to canonical positives/negatives before inference, tightening the decision boundary.

## Ensemble & Calibration
Models were stacked incrementally using a hierarchical blending schedule with geometric decay to prevent dominance by any single model. All scores were rank-normalized per rule to align calibration across retrieval, NLI, and LLM outputs. Final probabilities were clipped to [1e-6, 1−1e-6] to stabilize predictions.
competition_flow: Raw text data was cleaned and deduplicated, then processed through a diverse ensemble of embedding-based classifiers, NLI-framed DeBERTa, and LoRA-finetuned LLMs with test-time training and asymmetric few-shot prompting, with outputs rank-normalized per rule and hierarchically blended before final probability clipping.
data_processing: Removed exact duplicates reducing train size to <10k
Clustered and down-weighted near-duplicates per rule (cosine > 0.95–0.97)
Applied dynamic positive/negative balancing (~50/50, capped at 32×) during TTT
Applied per-rule rank-normalization and probability clipping to [1e-6, 1−1e-6]
cv_strategy: 6-fold cross-validation for embedding head hyperparameter tuning; public leaderboard used for final model selection and ensemble weighting.
ensembling: Models were stacked incrementally using a hierarchical blending schedule with geometric decay, with all scores rank-normalized per rule to align calibration across paradigms before final probability clipping to [1e-6, 1−1e-6].
insights: Dropping the `subreddit` field removed noisy signal and improved prediction consistency.
Exact deduplication reduced training size to under 10k examples without measurable LB drop, significantly speeding up training.
Asymmetric few-shot example selection using cosine similarity to canonical positives and negatives noticeably tightens the classification decision boundary.
Per-rule rank-normalization effectively removes calibration drift when blending models from different paradigms like retrieval, NLI, and LLMs.
Hierarchical blending with geometric decay naturally regularizes the ensemble and prevents single-model dominance.
critical_findings: Removing exact duplicates shrank the training set to under 10k examples without any measurable drop on the leaderboard, proving redundancy was harming efficiency rather than performance.
Asymmetric few-shot prompting (selecting the most similar positives and least similar negatives) yielded a +0.006 gain over vanilla few-shot by tightening the decision boundary.
Clustering and down-weighting near-duplicates within each rule prevented embedding classifiers from overfitting to repeated textual patterns.
